segments.title: Base-bleed shells for destroyer guns
segments.question: What impact would base-bleed shells have on Second World War vessels such as Tribal-class destroyers using Royal Navy 4.7-inch guns?
segments.answerShort: The answer says base-bleed rounds could extend range, but the tradeoff in explosive filler and poor long-range hit probability means the benefit is uncertain and may not be worth it.
segments.summary: A Bruships 238 Q&A weighs base-bleed shells for Royal Navy 4.7-inch destroyer guns against explosive filler loss, range gains, and practical hit probability.
segments.body: This live Q&A segment captures a technical gunnery exchange from Bruships 238. Dr. Clarke and the panel identify base-bleed ammunition as a range-extending approach that reduces drag rather than adding rocket thrust, then weigh the possible five-to-fifteen kilometer range gain against reduced explosive filler and the limited value of very long-range destroyer gunfire.

evidence.note: The audience prompt asks about base-bleed shells on World War II vessels such as Tribal-class destroyers and Royal Navy 4.7-inch guns.
The answer identifies base-bleed shells as gas-releasing rounds that maintain velocity and extend range, while warning about lost explosive filler.
The panel distinguishes base bleed from rocket assistance and estimates a five-to-fifteen kilometer range increase, again noting the explosive-mass tradeoff.
The answer questions whether destroyers need that much extra range, notes low hit probability at extreme range, and treats improved penetration as theoretically interesting but uncertain in practice.
